docs.title: RayleighBenard
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Climate geophysics

Low-order Rayleigh–Bénard convection model (Saltzman truncation).

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Yanagita & Kaneko (1995), Physica D 82, 288-313

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= - a y_{0} + a y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= r y_{1} - y_{0} y_{2} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= - b y_{2} + y_{0} y_{1} \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: SaltonSea
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Climate geophysics

Salton Sea eco-epidemiological (fish–bird disease) model.

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Upadhyay, Bairagi, Kundu & Chattopadhyay (2008), Appl. Math. Comput. 196, 392-401

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= - lam y_{0} y_{1} + r y_{0} \left(1 - \frac{y_{0} + y_{1}}{k}\right) \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= lam y_{0} y_{1} - \frac{m y_{1} y_{2}}{a + y_{1}} - \mu y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= - d y_{2} + \frac{th y_{1} y_{2}}{a + y_{1}} \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a 15 d 8.3 k 400 lam 0.06 m 15.5 mu 3.4 r 22 th 10.0

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.SaltonSea()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

docs.title: BurkeShaw

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Shaw (1981), Z. Naturforsch. A 36, 80-112

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= - n y_{0} - n y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= - n y_{0} y_{2} + y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= e + n y_{0} y_{1} \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: Equations

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: ArnoldWeb
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Exotic systems

Quasi-integrable Hamiltonian exhibiting the Arnold web.

Dimension: 5 · Reference: Froeschlé, Guzzo & Lega (2000), Science 289, 2108

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= - \frac{\mu \sin{\left(y_{2} \right)}}{\left(\cos{\left(y_{2} \right)} + \cos{\left(y_{3} \right)} + \cos{\left(y_{4} \right)} + 4\right)^{2}} \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= - \frac{\mu \sin{\left(y_{3} \right)}}{\left(\cos{\left(y_{2} \right)} + \cos{\left(y_{3} \right)} + \cos{\left(y_{4} \right)} + 4\right)^{2}} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= y_{0} \\ \dot{y_{3}} &= y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{4}} &= w \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default mu 0.01 w 1

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.ArnoldWeb()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

Back to Exotic systems

docs.title: CellularNeuralNetwork
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Exotic systems

Chaotic cellular neural network (CNN).

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Arena, Caponetto, Fortuna & Porto (1998), Int. J. Bifurc. Chaos 8, 1527

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= - 0.5 b \left(- \left|{y_{1} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{1} + 1}\right|\right) - 0.5 b \left(- \left|{y_{2} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{2} + 1}\right|\right) + 0.5 d \left(- \left|{y_{0} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{0} + 1}\right|\right) - y_{0} \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= - 0.5 a \left(- \left|{y_{2} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{2} + 1}\right|\right) - 0.5 b \left(- \left|{y_{0} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{0} + 1}\right|\right) + 0.5 c \left(- \left|{y_{1} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{1} + 1}\right|\right) - y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= 0.5 a \left(- \left|{y_{1} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{1} + 1}\right|\right) - 0.5 b \left(- \left|{y_{0} - 1}\right| + \left|{y_{0} + 1}\right|\right) - y_{2} - 0.5 \left|{y_{2} - 1}\right| + 0.5 \left|{y_{2} + 1}\right| \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a 4.4 b 3.21 c 1.1 d 1.24

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.CellularNeuralNetwork()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

docs.title: AnishchenkoAstakhov
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Oscillatory systems

Anishchenko–Astakhov oscillator — a radiophysical generator of spiral chaos.

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Anishchenko et al. (2007), Nonlinear Dynamics of Chaotic and Stochastic Systems

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} \dot{y_{0}} &= \mu y_{0} - y_{0} y_{2} + y_{1} \\ \dot{y_{1}} &= - y_{0} \\ \dot{y_{2}} &= \frac{\eta y_{0}^{2} \left(\operatorname{sign}{\left(y_{0} \right)} + 1\right)}{2} - \eta y_{2} \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default eta 0.5 mu 1.2

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:

sys = ts.systems.AnishchenkoAstakhov()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

docs.title: GrayScott
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Spatial fields

Gray–Scott reaction-diffusion on a periodic N x N grid (method of lines).

Dimension: variable · Reference: Pearson (1993), Science 261, 189-192

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _equations(Y, t, *, N, Du, Dv, F, k):
    # State layout: [u (N*N), v (N*N)]; unit grid spacing (h = 1).
    def uidx(r, c):
        return (r % N) * N + (c % N)

    def vidx(r, c):
        return N * N + (r % N) * N + (c % N)

    rhs = []
    # u-block.
    for r in range(N):
        for c in range(N):
            u0 = Y(uidx(r, c))
            v0 = Y(vidx(r, c))
            lap_u = (
                Y(uidx(r + 1, c))
                + Y(uidx(r - 1, c))
                + Y(uidx(r, c + 1))
                + Y(uidx(r, c - 1))
                - 4 * u0
            )
            rhs.append(Du * lap_u - u0 * v0 * v0 + F * (1 - u0))
    # v-block.
    for r in range(N):
        for c in range(N):
            u0 = Y(uidx(r, c))
            v0 = Y(vidx(r, c))
            lap_v = (
                Y(vidx(r + 1, c))
                + Y(vidx(r - 1, c))
                + Y(vidx(r, c + 1))
                + Y(vidx(r, c - 1))
                - 4 * v0
            )
            rhs.append(Dv * lap_v + u0 * v0 * v0 - (F + k) * v0)
    return rhs

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default N 48 Du 0.16 Dv 0.08 F 0.06 k 0.062

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.GrayScott()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

Back to Spatial fields

docs.title: SwiftHohenberg
docs.text:
Systems · Continuous · Spatial fields

Swift–Hohenberg pattern formation on a periodic N x N grid (method of lines).

Dimension: variable · Reference: Swift & Hohenberg (1977), Phys. Rev. A 15, 319-328

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _equations(Y, t, *, N, L, r):
    h = L / N
    inv_h2 = 1.0 / (h * h)
    inv_h4 = inv_h2 * inv_h2

    def idx(rr, cc):
        return (rr % N) * N + (cc % N)

    rhs = []
    for rr in range(N):
        for cc in range(N):

            def u(dr, dc, rr=rr, cc=cc):
                return Y(idx(rr + dr, cc + dc))

            u0 = u(0, 0)
            lap = (u(1, 0) + u(-1, 0) + u(0, 1) + u(0, -1) - 4 * u0) * inv_h2
            # Compact 13-point biharmonic (∇⁴) stencil on a periodic grid.
            bih = (
                20 * u0
                - 8 * (u(1, 0) + u(-1, 0) + u(0, 1) + u(0, -1))
                + 2 * (u(1, 1) + u(1, -1) + u(-1, 1) + u(-1, -1))
                + (u(2, 0) + u(-2, 0) + u(0, 2) + u(0, -2))
            ) * inv_h4
            # u_t = r u - (1 + ∇²)² u - u³ = r u - (u + 2 ∇²u + ∇⁴u) - u³
            rhs.append(r * u0 - (u0 + 2.0 * lap + bih) - u0 * u0 * u0)
    return rhs

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default N 32 L 40.0 r 0.3

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.SwiftHohenberg()
traj = sys.integrate(final_time=100.0, dt=0.01)

Back to Spatial fields

docs.text:

Chirikov standard (kicked-rotor) map.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Chirikov (1979), Phys. Rep. 52, 263-379

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, k):
    p, x = X
    pp = p + k * np.sin(x)
    xp = x + pp
    return pp, xp

docs.title: FoldedTowel
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Chaotic maps

Rössler folded-towel map — the prototypical hyperchaotic map.

Dimension: 3 · Reference: Rössler (1979), 'Chaotic oscillations: an example of hyperchaos', Lectures in Applied Mathematics 17, 141-156

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} y_{0}' &= a y_{0} \left(1 - y_{0}\right) - b \left(1 - 2 y_{2}\right) \left(c + y_{1}\right) \\ y_{1}' &= d \left(- e y_{0} + 1\right) \left(\left(c + y_{1}\right) \left(2 y_{2} + 1\right) - 1\right) \\ y_{2}' &= f y_{2} \left(1 - y_{2}\right) + g y_{1} \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a 3.8 b 0.05 c 0.35 d 0.1 e 1.9 f 3.78 g 0.2

docs.title: Usage

docs.title: Ikeda
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Chaotic maps

Ikeda (1979) map — laser ring cavity model.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Ikeda (1979), Opt. Commun. 30, 257-261

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, a, b, u):
    x, y = X
    t = a - b / (1 + x**2 + y**2)
    xp = 1 + u * (x * np.cos(t) - y * np.sin(t))
    yp = u * (x * np.sin(t) + y * np.cos(t))
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a 0.4 b 6.0 u 0.9

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Ikeda()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

Back to Chaotic maps

docs.title: Zaslavskii
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Chaotic maps

Zaslavsky dissipative standard map.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Zaslavsky (1978), Phys. Lett. A 69, 145-147

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, eps, nu, r):
    x, y = X
    mu = (1 - np.exp(-r)) / r
    xp = x + nu * (1 + mu * y) + eps * nu * mu * np.cos(2 * np.pi * x)
    # Phase variable lives on the unit circle: wrap mod 1 (floor-mod already
    # yields a result in [0, 1)).
    xp = xp % 1
    yp = np.exp(-r) * (y + eps * np.cos(2 * np.pi * x))
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default eps 5.0 nu 0.2 r 2.0

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Zaslavskii()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

Back to Chaotic maps

Systems · Discrete

system dim summary Bedhead 2 Bedhead attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map. Bogdanov 2 Bogdanov map -- discrete-time normal form of the Bogdanov-Takens bifurcation. GumowskiMira 2 Gumowski-Mira map -- a recurrence with a rational nonlinearity. Hopalong 2 Hopalong (Martin) attractor map -- a square-root strange-attractor map. Pickover 2 Pickover (Clifford) attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map. Svensson 2 Svensson attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map. ZeraouliaSprott 2 Zeraoulia-Sprott map -- a minimal 2-D rational chaotic map.

docs.title: Bedhead
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Exotic maps

Bedhead attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map.

Dimension: 2

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, a, b):
    x, y = X
    xp = np.sin(x * y / b) * y + np.cos(a * x - y)
    yp = x + np.sin(y) / b
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a -0.67 b 0.83

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Bedhead()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

docs.text:

docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} y_{0}' &= k y_{0} \left(y_{0} - 1\right) + \mu y_{0} y_{1} + y_{0} + y_{1} \left(eps + 1\right) \\ y_{1}' &= k y_{0} \left(y_{0} - 1\right) + \mu y_{0} y_{1} + y_{1} \left(eps + 1\right) \end{aligned} \]

docs.title: GumowskiMira
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Exotic maps

Gumowski-Mira map -- a recurrence with a rational nonlinearity.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Gumowski & Mira (1980), Recurrences and Discrete Dynamic Systems

docs.title: Equations
docs.text: \[ \begin{aligned} y_{0}' &= a y_{0} + b y_{1} + \frac{y_{0}^{2} \left(2 - 2 a\right)}{y_{0}^{2} + 1} \\ y_{1}' &= a \left(a y_{0} + b y_{1} + \frac{y_{0}^{2} \left(2 - 2 a\right)}{y_{0}^{2} + 1}\right) - y_{0} + \frac{\left(2 - 2 a\right) \left(a y_{0} + b y_{1} + \frac{y_{0}^{2} \left(2 - 2 a\right)}{y_{0}^{2} + 1}\right)^{2}}{\left(a y_{0} + b y_{1} + \frac{y_{0}^{2} \left(2 - 2 a\right)}{y_{0}^{2} + 1}\right)^{2} + 1} \end{aligned} \]

docs.text: parameter default a -1.1 b -0.2

docs.title: Hopalong
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Exotic maps

Hopalong (Martin) attractor map -- a square-root strange-attractor map.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Dewdney (1986), Scientific American 255(3), 14-20

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, a, b, c):
    x, y = X
    xp = y - 1 - np.sqrt(np.abs(b * x - 1 - c)) * np.sign(x - 1)
    yp = a - x - 1
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a 3.1 b 2.5 c 4.2

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Hopalong()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

Back to Exotic maps

docs.title: Pickover
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Exotic maps

Pickover (Clifford) attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Pickover (1990), Computers, Pattern, Chaos and Beauty (St. Martin's Press)

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, a, b, c, d):
    x, y = X
    xp = np.sin(a * y) + c * np.cos(a * x)
    yp = np.sin(b * x) + d * np.cos(b * y)
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default a -1.4 b 1.6 c 1.0 d 0.7

docs.title: Usage

sys = ts.systems.Pickover()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

docs.title: Svensson
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Exotic maps

Svensson attractor -- a sinusoidal planar strange-attractor map.

Dimension: 2

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, a, b, c, d):
    x, y = X
    xp = d * np.sin(a * x) - np.sin(b * y)
    yp = c * np.cos(a * x) + np.cos(b * y)
    return xp, yp

docs.text: parameter default a 1.5 b -1.8 c 1.6 d 0.9

docs.title: Geometric maps
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete

4 systems in this category.

system dim summary Baker 2 Baker's map, an area-preserving stretch-cut-stack map of the unit square. Chebyshev 1 Chebyshev map, the Chebyshev polynomial iterated as a dynamical system. Circle 1 Arnold's (standard) circle map of the unit circle onto itself. Tent 1 Tent map, a piecewise-linear unimodal map on the unit interval.

docs.title: Baker
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Geometric maps

Baker's map, an area-preserving stretch-cut-stack map of the unit square.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Hopf (1937), Ergodentheorie (Springer, Berlin)

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, alpha):
    """
    Right-hand side of the Baker map.

    x, y: Current state variables
    alpha: Fraction determining the fold (0 < alpha < 1)

    Written branchlessly with ``np.where`` (rather than a Python ``if`` on
    the state) so the step traces to a straight-line tape and runs on the
    Rust engine.  On the attractor ``y in [0, 1)`` the single test
    ``y < alpha`` is equivalent to ``0 <= y < alpha``.
    """
    x, y = X
    lower = y < alpha
    xp = np.where(lower, (2 * x) % 1, (2 * x - 1) % 1)  # stretch / shift in x
    yp = np.where(lower, y / alpha, (y - alpha) / (1 - alpha))  # fold in y
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default alpha 0.5

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Baker()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

Back to Geometric maps

docs.title: Chebyshev
docs.text:

Dimension: 1 · Reference: Adler & Rivlin (1964), Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 15, 794-796

def _step(X, a):

docs.title: Circle
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Geometric maps

Arnold's (standard) circle map of the unit circle onto itself.

Dimension: 1 · Reference: Arnold (1965), Amer. Math. Soc. Transl. 46, 213-284

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, omega, k):
    theta = X
    thetap = theta + omega + (k / (2 * np.pi)) * np.sin(2 * np.pi * theta)
    return thetap % 1

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default omega 0.333 k 5.7

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.Circle()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

docs.text:

Peter de Jong attractor map.

def _step(X, a, b, c, d):
    x, y = X
    xp = np.sin(a * y) - np.cos(b * x)

docs.title: KaplanYorke
docs.text:
Systems · Discrete · Polynomial maps

Kaplan–Yorke map.

Dimension: 2 · Reference: Kaplan & Yorke (1979), Functional Differential Equations and Approximation of Fixed Points, Lecture Notes in Mathematics 730, 204-227

docs.title: Equations
docs.text:
@staticmethod
def _step(X, alpha):
    x, y = X
    # Doubling map on the unit circle. The canonical wrap is mod 1, but the
    # pure doubling map x -> frac(2x) drains a float mantissa one bit per
    # step (2x is an exact shift) and collapses to the x=0 fixed point in
    # ~52 iterations, killing the chaos. Wrapping just below 1 injects a
    # ~5e-8 offset at each fold that keeps the orbit non-degenerate; the
    # dynamics (and Lyapunov exponent ln 2) are unchanged to that tolerance.
    xp = (2 * x) % 0.99999995
    yp = alpha * y + np.cos(4 * np.pi * x)
    return xp, yp

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: parameter default alpha 0.2

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import tsdynamics as ts

sys = ts.systems.KaplanYorke()
traj = sys.iterate(steps=10_000)

Back to Polynomial maps
